content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Mark Sellers 
City Attorney 
Thousand Oaks 
401 West Hillcrest 
Thousand Oaks, CA 
Dear Mr. Sellers: 
T ...nnica' Assistance 
(916) 322·$662 
Drive 
91360 
Administration 
322·.5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
October 29, 1984 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. A-84-270 
This letter is to confirm my advice to you on October 25, 
1984. 
FACTS 
A business entity which operates movie theaters provided 
each member of the Thousand Oaks' City Council with free movie 
passes. 
The passes allow two persons to attend movies by paying 
only $1 per person. 
The City of Thousand Oaks is located 15 
miles from two theaters operated by the business entity. 
One of 
the theaters is a drive-in which changes its movies on a weekly 
basis and charges $3.75 per person. 
The other theater is a 
walk-in which changes its movies 18 times a year and charges $5 
per person. 
The business entity which owns the theater may come 
before the City Council with zoning decisions relating to a 
shopping center that it is constructing in Thousand Oaks. 
QUESTION PRESENTED 
What is the value of the movie passes? 


Mark Sellers 
October 29, 1984 
Page 2 
DISCUSSION 
A free movie theater pass constitutes a "gift n to the public 
official receiving it. 
(Government Code Section 82028. 1/) 
The pass must be reported on the official's Statement of 
Economic Interests if its value is $50 or more. 
If the value of 
the pass is $250 or more, the official must disqualify himself 
or herself from any decision which will foreseeably have a 
material financial effect on the source of the pass. 
(Sections 
87100, 87103.) 
During our telephone conversation, we determined 
that the value of the pass is approximately $150. 
This is 
sufficient to require that the City Councilmembers disclose the 
pass, but it is insufficient to require their disqualification 
on decisions affecting the business entity which provided the 
passes. 
If you have any questions, please feel free to contact me at 
(916) 322-5901. 
JSM:plh 
Very truly yours, 
:Jeuu:s <5~~ 
Janis Shank McLean 
Staff Attorney 
Legal Division 
1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

sections.question_synthetic: The City Attorney seeks FPPC guidance on how to value free movie theater passes provided by a business entity to Thousand Oaks City Council members, particularly to determine disclosure and disqualification obligations under California political conduct law.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined the movie passes are valued at approximately $150, which triggers disclosure requirements on Statements of Economic Interests but does not mandate disqualification from decisions affecting the business entity providing the passes (which would require a $250+ value).
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Clear advice letter format with standard sections. All key information successfully extracted. Minor OCR artifact ('T...nnica' likely 'Technical'). Question and conclusion sections are clearly delineated.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What is the value of the movie passes? 


Mark Sellers 
October 29, 1984

CONCLUSION: During our telephone conversation, we determined that the value of the pass is approximately $150. This is sufficient to require that the City Councilmembers disclose the pass, but it is insufficient to require their disqualification on decisions affecting the business entity which provided the passes.

embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter A-84-270 addresses the valuation and reporting obligations for free movie theater passes provided by a business entity to Thousand Oaks City Council members. The FPPC determined the passes are valued at $150, requiring disclosure but not disqualification from council decisions affecting the business.
